section.title: Praise for Deliverance
section.sectionRationale: Within the Isaiah apocalypse, chapters 24 to 27, this chapter answers the devastation of chapter 24 with a song of praise. The prophet turns from describing a shattered earth to worship, and the LORD's plan reaches its high point: a banquet on Mount Zion for all peoples and the swallowing up of death itself, a promise later quoted in 1 Corinthians 15:54 of the resurrection.
section.thesis: The LORD who humbled ruthless cities becomes a refuge for the poor and needy, and on His mountain He prepares a feast for all peoples while swallowing up death forever, wiping every tear away, so that His waiting people can say at last, this is our God, we have waited for Him.
section.frameNote: Five movements: praise for the LORD's faithful wonders and the ruined fortress of strangers; the LORD as refuge and stronghold for the poor; the banquet on the mountain and the swallowing up of death; the joyful confession, this is our God; and the LORD's hand resting on Zion while pride is brought low.

units.label: Praise for the LORD's faithful wonders

verses.text: O LORD, You are my God! I will exalt You; I will praise Your name. For You have worked wonders, plans formed long ago, in perfect faithfulness.
Indeed, You have made the city a heap of rubble, the fortified town a ruin. The fortress of strangers is a city no more; it will never be rebuilt.
Therefore, a strong people will honor You. The cities of ruthless nations will revere You.

units.label: A refuge for the poor and needy
units.note: shade from the heat, shelter from the storm

verses.text: For You have been a refuge for the poor, a stronghold for the needy in distress, a refuge from the storm, a shade from the heat. For the breath of the ruthless is like rain against a wall,
like heat in a dry land. You subdue the uproar of foreigners. As the shade of a cloud cools the heat, so the song of the ruthless is silenced.

units.label: A banquet on the mountain, death swallowed up

verses.text: On this mountain the LORD of Hosts will prepare a {{mishteh|banquet}} for all the peoples, a feast of aged wine, of choice meat, of finely aged wine.
On this mountain He will {{bala|swallow up}} the shroud that enfolds all peoples, the sheet that covers all nations;
He will swallow up death forever. The Lord GOD will wipe away the tears from every face and remove the disgrace of His people from the whole earth. For the LORD has spoken.

units.label: This is our God, we have waited for Him

verses.text: And in that day it will be said, “Surely this is our God; we have waited for Him, and He has saved us. This is the LORD for whom we have waited. Let us rejoice and be glad in His salvation.”

units.label: The LORD's hand rests on Zion, pride brought low
units.note: Moab as an example of humbled pride

verses.text: For the hand of the LORD will rest on this mountain. But Moab will be trampled in his place as straw is trodden into the dung pile.
He will spread out his hands within it, as a swimmer spreads his arms to swim. His pride will be brought low, despite the skill of his hands.
The high-walled fortress will be brought down, cast to the ground, into the dust.

mishteh.gloss: A feast or banquet, often marking celebration, covenant, or royal favor. The LORD's banquet here is for all peoples, not Israel alone, a rich foreshadowing of the messianic banquet and the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9.

bala.gloss: To swallow up, devour, or engulf completely. Used twice in quick succession, first of the shroud of mourning over all peoples and then, climactically, of death itself, a word Paul quotes directly in 1 Corinthians 15:54 to describe Christ's resurrection victory.

basic.context: After the devastation of chapter 24, Isaiah bursts into personal praise, celebrating that the LORD has ruined a proud, oppressive fortress and become instead a refuge for the poor and needy. The chapter reaches its high point in a breathtaking promise: on His mountain, the LORD will host a banquet for all peoples and swallow up death itself forever, wiping every tear from every face.
basic.hermeneutics: The ruined city and fortress likely reflect a real historical humbling of an oppressive power, while the banquet and the swallowing up of death, verses 6 to 8, reach beyond any single historical event toward God's final, universal salvation. Paul's use of verse 8 in 1 Corinthians 15:54 confirms that its ultimate fulfillment is the bodily resurrection secured in Christ, even as its first hearers likely heard a nearer hope of rescue as well.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD will bala, swallow up, first the shroud of mourning covering the nations and then death itself, the very word Paul quotes when celebrating Christ's resurrection victory, 1 Corinthians 15:54. This same mountain hosts a mishteh, banquet, for all peoples, not Israel alone, hinting at a salvation with a scope as wide as the death it overturns.
basic.typology: The banquet the LORD prepares on His mountain for all peoples anticipates the messianic feast Jesus repeatedly pictures in His parables and the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9. The swallowing up of death and the wiping away of every tear anticipate the new creation described in Revelation 21:4, both drawing directly on this chapter's language.
basic.christ: Paul explicitly quotes verse 8, death is swallowed up in victory, in 1 Corinthians 15:54, tying this ancient promise directly to Christ's bodily resurrection and the future resurrection of all who trust in Him. The banquet for all peoples anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb, and the wiping away of every tear is picked up nearly word for word in Revelation 21:4, showing that Christ personally accomplishes what this chapter promises.
basic.god: God is a refuge for the poor and needy precisely because He first humbles ruthless, oppressive power; His strength serves the weak rather than crushing them further. His salvation reaches beyond any one nation to a banquet, a shroud lifted, and death swallowed up for all peoples, revealing a heart set on the widest possible rescue.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15:54-57 quotes Isaiah 25:8 directly as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection victory over death. Revelation 21:4 echoes wiping away every tear in the description of the new creation. Revelation 19:9 pictures the marriage supper of the Lamb, the fulfillment of this chapter's banquet for all peoples. Luke 14:15-24 records Jesus' own parable of a great banquet open to all who will come.
basic.oneStory: This chapter moves from praise for a humbled oppressor to the LORD's astonishing promise of a banquet for all peoples and the swallowing up of death itself. It is one of the Old Testament's clearest advance glimpses of the gospel, fulfilled when Christ rose from the dead and secured the resurrection hope and endless feast this chapter foresees.

context.literary: A hymn of praise structured around contrast: a ruined, ruthless fortress giving way to a refuge for the poor, and a shroud of mourning giving way to a banquet and the end of death itself, closing with a joyful confession of trust and a final image of pride, embodied in Moab, brought low.
context.historical: The unnamed ruined city, verse 2, may recall various historical humblings of oppressive powers Israel witnessed, while the mention of Moab, verse 10, situates the chapter within the same regional world addressed by the earlier oracles against the nations. Its banquet and resurrection language, however, reaches well beyond any single eighth century event.
context.canonical: One of the Old Testament's clearest and earliest anticipations of bodily resurrection and final victory over death, directly quoted by Paul in 1 Corinthians 15 and echoed by John in Revelation 21, making this chapter a crucial bridge between Old Testament hope and New Testament fulfillment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To move worshipers from praise for a specific act of God's justice toward the far greater hope of a universal banquet and the final undoing of death itself, assuring God's people that their long waiting for Him will end in salvation, not disappointment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's redemptive plan and its ultimate goal rather than prescribing a specific ritual or timeline; its abiding truth is the certainty of God's final victory over death and His invitation to all peoples, not a blueprint for exactly how or when each detail unfolds historically.

debates.question: How much of the banquet and the swallowing up of death, verses 6 to 8, was understood or fulfilled in Isaiah's own time, and how much awaits final consummation?

views.label: Primarily an eschatological promise, understood only in shadow by its first hearers
views.case: The scope, all peoples, and the totality of the promise, death swallowed up forever, exceed any plausible near historical fulfillment, suggesting the original audience grasped only a partial hope that the New Testament later unveils fully in Christ.

views.label: A promise with a real, if partial, near fulfillment alongside its ultimate horizon
views.case: Prophetic literature often layers a near, historical rescue, here perhaps deliverance from a specific oppressor, together with a further, final fulfillment, meaning the first hearers could have experienced genuine relief that also functioned as a partial down payment on the fuller promise.

debates.bearing: However one weighs the near and far horizons, the New Testament's direct citation of verse 8 regarding the resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:54, settles that its ultimate and complete fulfillment is found in Christ, whatever partial comfort earlier believers may have also drawn from it.

study.originalLanguages: The verb bala, swallow up, appears twice in close succession, first devouring the shroud of mourning over the nations and then, in escalating force, devouring death itself, the very verse Paul later quotes triumphantly in 1 Corinthians 15:54. The mishteh, banquet, prepared on this mountain is explicitly for all peoples, widening Israel's hope for salvation into a vision embracing every nation.

typology.type: The banquet on the mountain for all peoples
typology.fulfillment: The marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9, and the messianic banquet Jesus repeatedly pictures in His parables, Luke 14:15-24.

typology.type: The swallowing up of death and the wiping away of every tear
typology.fulfillment: Christ's bodily resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57, and the final removal of death, mourning, and tears in the new creation, Revelation 21:4.

study.christ: Paul quotes Isaiah 25:8 directly, death is swallowed up in victory, and applies it to the resurrection Christ has already accomplished and will complete for all who belong to Him, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57. The banquet for all peoples on God's mountain anticipates the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9, and the wiping away of every tear is picked up almost word for word in Revelation 21:4 as the crowning promise of the new creation. What Isaiah glimpsed and sang about, Christ has secured in history through His own death and resurrection.
study.god: God's strength is displayed not merely in humbling the proud but in becoming, in the very same movement, a refuge and stronghold for the poor and needy who have nowhere else to turn. His redemptive ambition is as wide as death itself and as wide as all peoples, and He is faithful to plans formed long ago, so that waiting on Him, however long, ends in salvation rather than shame.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Isaiah 25:8 directly, declaring that Christ's resurrection has swallowed up death in victory.
Echoes wiping away every tear from every face as the crowning promise of the new creation.
The marriage supper of the Lamb, the fulfillment of this chapter's banquet prepared for all peoples.
Jesus' parable of a great banquet open to all who will come, echoing this chapter's universal invitation.

study.oneStory: This chapter moves from praising God for humbling a ruthless power to the staggering hope of a banquet for all peoples and death itself swallowed up forever. It is one of the clearest Old Testament windows into the gospel, fulfilled when Christ rose from the dead, guaranteeing that everyone who waits on Him will finally say, this is our God, we have waited for Him, and He has saved us.

sermon.title: Cities of Ruthless Nations Will Revere Thee
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly through Isaiah 25:1 to 8, drawing out how the LORD's humbling of ruthless power and His becoming a refuge for the poor lead to the promise of the banquet and the swallowing up of death, the same movement this study traces to its fulfillment in Christ.

quiz.q: Why does Isaiah praise the LORD in verses 1 to 3?

options.text: Because the LORD, in perfect faithfulness, has ruined a ruthless, oppressive fortress.
options.note: Verses 1 to 2 give exactly this reason for praise, God's faithful, planned action against oppression.

options.text: Because Israel has just won a major military victory on her own.
options.note: The praise is directed to the LORD's own action, not a self-achieved Israelite military victory.

options.text: Because a new temple has just been completed.
options.note: Not mentioned in this passage; the praise concerns the LORD ruining an oppressive city.

options.text: Because a famine has finally ended.
options.note: Not the stated reason here; the specific cause for praise is the humbling of a ruthless fortress.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise to prepare on His mountain in verse 6?

options.text: A banquet, a feast of aged wine and choice meat, for all peoples.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly describes this banquet, and specifies it is for all peoples, not Israel alone.

options.text: A courtroom trial for all the nations.
options.note: Not the image in this verse; the picture is a celebratory feast, not a trial.

options.text: A military parade celebrating conquest.
options.note: Not described here; the specific image given is a banquet.

options.text: A private feast for Israel's priests only.
options.note: The text specifically says for all peoples, not a priests-only or Israel-only banquet.

quiz.q: What astonishing promise appears in verse 8, later quoted by Paul?

options.text: He will swallow up death forever and wipe away every tear.
options.note: Verse 8 states this directly, and Paul quotes it in 1 Corinthians 15:54.

options.text: He will make death more frequent among the wicked.
options.note: The opposite; the promise is the total abolition of death, not its increase.

options.text: He will extend human lifespans by a few years.
options.note: Not the promise here; the text speaks of swallowing up death entirely, not merely extending life somewhat.

options.text: He will remove death only for the wealthy and powerful.
options.note: The promise applies to His people generally, removing disgrace from the whole earth, not a wealth-restricted group.

quiz.q: What is the joyful confession the people make in verse 9?

options.text: Surely this is our God; we have waited for Him, and He has saved us.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this exact confession of long-awaited salvation.

options.text: We no longer need to trust in the LORD.
options.note: The opposite; the confession affirms continued trust and celebrates His salvation.

options.text: We built this salvation ourselves through our own strength.
options.note: The confession credits the LORD's salvation, not human achievement.

options.text: We regret having waited so long for the LORD.
options.note: The tone is joyful vindication of their waiting, not regret.

quiz.q: How does Isaiah 25:8 connect directly to the New Testament?

options.text: Paul quotes it in 1 Corinthians 15:54 as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection victory over death.
options.note: Paul explicitly cites this verse to celebrate the resurrection Christ has secured.

options.text: It is never referenced anywhere in the New Testament.
options.note: The opposite; it is directly and explicitly quoted by Paul.

options.text: It is quoted only in a minor, disputed manuscript variant.
options.note: It is quoted plainly and centrally in the widely accepted text of 1 Corinthians 15.

options.text: It is referenced only indirectly through vague thematic parallels.
options.note: The connection is a direct quotation, not merely an indirect thematic echo.

questions: Isaiah praises God specifically for humbling a ruthless, oppressive power and becoming a refuge for the poor. Where in your own life or community do you need to trust God as a refuge against something that feels ruthless or overwhelming?
The LORD prepares His banquet for all peoples, not one nation alone. Is there a person or group you unconsciously assume is outside the reach of God's welcome?
Death is swallowed up forever and every tear wiped away, a promise fulfilled in Christ's resurrection. What grief or fear of death are you carrying that this promise speaks directly into?

The people's confession is, we have waited for Him, and He has saved us. What are you currently waiting on God for, and how does this chapter encourage you to keep waiting in hope rather than despair?
Moab's downfall came through pride, despite the skill of his hands. Where might your own competence or self-sufficiency be quietly standing in for trust in God?

translationNotes.note: Where BSB prints em dashes around plans formed long ago, they have been rendered as commas per house style.
The shroud or sheet covering all peoples is a poetic image for mourning and death's universal reach, immediately answered in verse 8 by death itself being swallowed up.
Moab appears here as a specific, representative example of proud human power humbled, tying this hymn of universal hope back to the regional oracles against the nations in earlier chapters.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 25:1-12. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style.
